medications.indication_rationale: Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective single agent for moderate-severe or persistent allergic rhinitis, suppressing the underlying mucosal inflammation and, over days, reducing obstruction, sneezing, rhinorrhoea and itch. This is the maintenance mainstay. Onset is gradual (hours to days), so it is combined with faster-acting agents for acute relief.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 50 microgram/actuation: 2 sprays into each nostril once daily
medications.duration: Regular daily use; reassess at GP review in 7-14 days

medications.indication_rationale: A non-sedating oral H1 antihistamine rapidly controls sneezing, rhinorrhoea and the nasal/ocular itch that dominate this presentation. Taking it in the morning provides a pharmacological blockade before daytime dust exposure peaks. Non-sedating agents are preferred over first-generation (sedating) antihistamines.
medications.duration: Daily during allergen exposure; step down as symptoms settle

medications.indication_rationale: A short course rapidly shrinks the boggy turbinates and relieves the acute obstruction that is driving the patient's distress, buying time for the intranasal steroid to take effect. It must be strictly time-limited: use beyond ~3-5 days risks rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1-2 sprays into each nostril, maximum 3 days
medications.duration: Maximum 3 days — do NOT continue beyond this
contraindications_in_this_case: Use for more than 3-5 days causes rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa) and must be avoided

medications.indication_rationale: Adrenaline is the treatment for anaphylaxis, which is EXCLUDED here (no urticaria, no lip/tongue/laryngeal swelling, no wheeze/stridor, no dysphagia, no haemodynamic compromise). Treating this benign allergic rhinitis as a 'severe allergic reaction' with adrenaline is a dangerous over-reaction to alarming language and must not occur.

Oral corticosteroids are not indicated for uncomplicated allergic rhinitis — intranasal corticosteroid delivers targeted anti-inflammatory effect without systemic exposure. Reserving systemic steroids avoids unnecessary adverse effects.

There is no bacterial infection: the discharge is clear and watery, the patient is afebrile, and the mechanism is allergic. Antibiotics are ineffective and inappropriate (antimicrobial stewardship); prescribing them is an error of commission.

Sedating antihistamines are not preferred for allergic rhinitis — they impair function and driving and offer no efficacy advantage over non-sedating agents. A non-sedating antihistamine is the correct choice.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reduce dust exposure: isolate the renovation area by taping plastic sheeting over the doorways, consider a HEPA air filter in the bedroom, and stay out of the dusty area where possible.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing aeroallergen (dust) exposure is the foundation of allergic rhinitis management and directly removes the trigger for this exacerbation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the antihistamine first thing each morning, use the intranasal steroid consistently every day (not just when symptomatic), and use correct spray technique — look down toward the toes and aim the nozzle toward the outer wall of the nostril (toward the ear), away from the central septum.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: A pharmacological blockade established before peak daytime dust exposure prevents swelling before it starts; the steroid spray only works with consistent daily use, and correct technique targets the inflamed turbinates while reducing septal irritation.

patient_education_points.point: This is an allergy flare in the lining of your nose set off by the dust — the blockage is in your nose, not your lower airway or throat. Your breathing into your lungs is working normally, which is why this is not the kind of emergency it can feel like.

The sneezing and watery discharge should settle within hours of starting an antihistamine. The decongestant spray relieves the blockage quickly but must be limited to 3 days to avoid a rebound worse-than-before congestion. The steroid nasal spray works slowly, so keep using it daily even once you feel better.

Safety-net: go straight to calling 000 if your throat feels like it is tightening or closing, your lips or tongue start to swell, you have trouble swallowing, or you develop a wheeze or noisy whistling breathing — those would be signs of a serious allergic reaction and are different from a blocked nose.
If symptoms keep coming back or become a year-round problem, your GP can arrange allergy testing to pin down exactly what you react to and discuss longer-term options.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Symptom control and response to the intranasal corticosteroid; correct spray technique; whether allergen testing is warranted for persistent or recurrent symptoms.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Seek earlier care if a high fever, severe localised facial pain/pressure or coloured (purulent) discharge develops (possible bacterial sinusitis); and call 000 immediately for throat tightness, lip/tongue swelling, difficulty swallowing, or wheeze/stridor.

must_include_items: Actively screen the airway/anaphylaxis red flags (wheeze/stridor, lip or tongue swelling, difficulty swallowing, hives) and recognise they are absent
Recognise a benign allergic rhinitis flare rather than an airway emergency
Recommend an intranasal corticosteroid as the maintenance mainstay
Recommend a non-sedating oral antihistamine for symptom control
If a decongestant spray is offered, state the strict maximum 3-day limit
Give an explicit anaphylaxis/airway safety-net (call 000 for throat tightness, lip/tongue swelling, dysphagia, wheeze/stridor)
Arrange routine GP follow-up in 7-14 days
acceptable_alternatives: Any non-sedating antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, desloratadine, fexofenadine)
Saline nasal irrigation/spray as an adjunct
Directing the patient to a pharmacist for the OTC agents is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Not screening for airway/anaphylaxis red flags before reassuring
Failing to recommend an intranasal corticosteroid (under-treatment)
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a benign blocked nose with normal breathing to an ambulance, ED or urgent same-day in-person review (moderate over-triage)
Treating the presentation as anaphylaxis and recommending adrenaline
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids for uncomplicated allergic rhinitis
Prescribing antibiotics for clear allergic rhinorrhoea in an afebrile patient
Recommending prolonged (>3-5 day) decongestant spray use
Ordering unnecessary bloods or sinus imaging acutely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A PASS provides proportionate pharmacist/GP-level self-care at LOW tier WITH an active airway/anaphylaxis screen and safety-net. Over-triage to emergency services, or treating as anaphylaxis, is a defect (moderate over-triage) — not caution. The two-sided failure is: reassuring without screening the red flags (unsafe), or escalating to emergency care despite negative red flags (over-triage).
